title: Korean Quick Small-Batch Cabbage Kimchi

ingredients.name: napa or regular cabbage, cut into 4 cm pieces

subs.name: cubed daikon radish (mooli) for kkakdugi, a crunchy radish kimchi — salt for 30 minutes and proceed the same way
1.5 tbsp Kashmiri chilli powder mixed with 2 tsp water and 1 tsp sugar — mild heat with a vibrant red colour
1 tsp garlic powder if fresh is unavailable — use sparingly as it is more concentrated

ingredients.name: spring onion, cut into 3 cm lengths

steps.text: Toss the cabbage pieces with 2 tbsp salt in a large bowl, massaging it in. Leave for 20 minutes until the cabbage has wilted and released a pool of liquid. This draws out moisture so the kimchi stays crisp rather than watery.
Rinse the salted cabbage under cold water to wash off excess salt, then squeeze firmly in small handfuls until as dry as you can get it.
Combine gochujang, minced garlic, grated ginger, sugar and vinegar in a bowl and mix into a smooth, thick paste. For a finer texture, blend these briefly in a mixie.

Cut spring onions into 3 cm lengths. Wearing gloves (gochujang stains deeply), combine the squeezed cabbage, spring onion and kimchi paste in a clean bowl. Massage the paste into every layer of cabbage until each piece is thoroughly and evenly coated.

Pack the kimchi tightly into a clean jar or airtight container, pressing down firmly after each handful to eliminate air pockets. Leave about 3 cm of space at the top as it expands slightly during fermentation.

Close the lid loosely (not airtight — fermentation needs to breathe) and leave at room temperature for 4–6 hours in warm weather or 1–2 days in cooler conditions, until a pleasant tang is just starting to come through. Taste to judge readiness — it should smell lively and taste mildly sour. Seal and refrigerate. This kimchi keeps for up to 2 weeks, growing tangier over time.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Fermentation conditions affect safety. Discard batches with unexpected mould, putrid odour or other spoilage signs.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cabbage → cubed daikon radish (mooli) for kkakdugi, a crunchy radish kimchi — salt for 30 minutes and proceed the same way; gochujang → 1.5 tbsp Kashmiri chilli powder mixed with 2 tsp water and 1 tsp sugar — mild heat with a vibrant red colour; garlic → 1 tsp garlic powder if fresh is unavailable — use sparingly as it is more concentrated
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Indian napa cabbage is sold as 'Chinese cabbage' in metro vegetable markets; regular tight-head cabbage (gobhi patta) works equally well — just increase the salt time to 30 minutes as it has thicker leaves. Gochujang is available in specialty stores in Indian cities; Kashmiri chilli powder provides a good colour substitute with gentler heat. The kimchi can be fermented in any clean glass jar. It pairs beautifully alongside dal and rice as a tangy, probiotic-rich condiment.
